## BQP Appoints NUAIR CEO Craig Marcinkowski to Board as U.S. Defense and Aerospace Growth Accelerates
‍
**SYRACUSE, N.Y. — June 12, 2026**
Originally published by: [Citybiz.co](https://www.citybiz.co/article/859754/bqp-appoints-nuair-ceo-craig-marcinkowski-to-board-as-u-s-defense-and-aerospace-growth-accelerates/)
BQP, a leader in quantum-inspired simulation and digital twin technologies for mission-critical engineering applications, today announced the appointment of aerospace and defense technology executive Craig Marcinkowski to its Board of Directors.
The appointment comes as BQP expands its engagement across the U.S. aerospace, defense, and advanced manufacturing sectors, driven by increasing demand for computational technologies that accelerate engineering design, optimization, and mission planning.
Marcinkowski currently serves as President and Chief Executive Officer of NUAIR, a nationally recognized organization advancing uncrewed systems, advanced air mobility, and aerospace innovation. Over the course of his career, he has held leadership roles at SRC Inc., Lockheed Martin, Saab Inc., and Fotokite, helping organizations develop, commercialize, and deploy advanced technologies across defense, surveillance, aerospace, and public safety markets.
His addition to the Board strengthens BQP's strategic leadership as the company scales deployments of its quantum-inspired engineering platform across industries where simulation performance, computational efficiency, and digital engineering capabilities are becoming increasingly critical.
## Supporting the Next Phase of Growth
BQP has experienced significant growth over the past year as organizations seek new approaches to address increasingly complex engineering and optimization challenges. The company is currently collaborating with the U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory and working alongside technology leaders including NVIDIA, MathWorks, IBM, Intel, as well as several Tier I aerospace and automotive organizations.
As engineering teams face growing computational demands across areas such as computational fluid dynamics, design optimization, mission planning, and machine learning, BQP's technology enables organizations to accelerate simulation and decision-making using today's computing infrastructure.
“BQP is operating at the intersection of several major technology transitions affecting engineering and national security,” said Marcinkowski. “Organizations are looking for ways to model, optimize, and validate increasingly complex systems without waiting for future hardware breakthroughs. BQP has developed an approach that delivers practical value today while positioning customers for the future of advanced computing. I’m excited to support the company during this next stage of growth.”
## Advancing Quantum-Inspired Engineering
Founded with the vision of bringing quantum-inspired computing into real-world engineering environments, BQP develops technologies that help organizations solve computationally intensive problems using existing CPU, GPU, and HPC infrastructure.
Its flagship platform, **BQPhy®** , combines quantum-inspired optimization, simulation, and digital twin capabilities to address bottlenecks in engineering workflows. The platform integrates with existing software environments and allows organizations to improve performance without disrupting established processes or waiting for large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computers.
“Craig brings deep expertise across aerospace, defense, advanced systems development, and technology commercialization,” said Abhishek Chopra, Founder, Chief Executive Officer, and Chief Scientific Officer of BQP. “His experience building organizations and guiding innovation in highly regulated and mission-critical industries will be invaluable as we continue expanding our presence across the U.S. market. We are pleased to welcome him to the Board during this important phase of growth.”
## Strengthening U.S. Aerospace and Defense Engagement
The appointment reflects BQP's continued investment in the aerospace and defense ecosystem, where digital engineering, simulation-driven design, and advanced optimization are becoming essential capabilities for maintaining technological advantage.
As governments and industry accelerate adoption of digital twins, autonomous systems, and AI-enabled engineering workflows, BQP is positioning its quantum-inspired technologies to help organizations solve increasingly complex challenges while leveraging existing computational infrastructure.
With Marcinkowski joining the Board, BQP further strengthens its leadership team as it expands collaborations with government agencies, research institutions, and industry partners focused on next-generation aerospace and defense innovation.

## How Regional Quantum Hubs Could Accelerate Commercialization in the US
**By Louis Castel,**  
Published on June 11, 2026
_Originally published on_[ _AZoQuantum_](https://www.azoquantum.com/article.aspx?ArticleID=715)
For years, the quantum industry has focused on a single question: When will the technology be ready?
A more important question is now emerging: When will the ecosystem be ready?
The challenge facing quantum technology is no longer limited to scientific breakthroughs. Across the industry, researchers are demonstrating new advances in quantum hardware, algorithms, networking, and simulation. Yet commercialization remains the defining bottleneck.
History shows that transformative technologies rarely succeed because of a single invention. They succeed because ecosystems emerge around them. Semiconductors required manufacturing clusters. Aerospace required supply chains and testing infrastructure. Artificial intelligence required cloud computing and developer communities.
Quantum will be no different.
The regions that successfully connect research institutions, startups, industry partners, government agencies, and investors will likely determine which innovations move beyond the laboratory and into real-world deployment.
That is why initiatives such as the Advancing Regional Quantum Hubs Act represent more than economic development programs. They represent a shift in how quantum commercialization is being approached in the United States.
## Why Geography Still Matters in Deep Technology
The digital economy has created the perception that innovation can happen anywhere.
In reality, deep technology industries still depend heavily on physical ecosystems.
Quantum development requires access to specialized talent, advanced computing resources, laboratory infrastructure, manufacturing expertise, and application-focused partnerships. Building these capabilities independently is difficult. Building them collectively accelerates progress.
Regional hubs create environments where researchers, engineers, startups, enterprise partners, and government organizations can work on common challenges while shortening the distance between discovery and deployment.
The value is not simply proximity. It is connectivity.
The faster ideas move between research, validation, commercialization, and adoption, the faster an industry matures.
## Why Upstate New York Is Emerging as a Quantum Powerhouse
Few regions illustrate this model more effectively than Upstate New York.
The region already possesses many of the ingredients required for quantum commercialization: advanced manufacturing capabilities, semiconductor expertise, defense infrastructure, high-performance computing resources, and world-class universities.
Equally important is the growing network connecting these capabilities.
Across the New York Thruway corridor, startups, research institutions, defense organizations, and enterprise technology companies are building an increasingly collaborative ecosystem. Instead of operating as isolated stakeholders, these organizations are contributing to a shared vision for quantum innovation.
That collaborative model may become one of the region's greatest competitive advantages.
Innovation accelerates when expertise flows freely across organizational boundaries.
## Commercialization Is a Collaboration Problem
Many discussions about quantum focus on hardware performance, qubit counts, or algorithmic breakthroughs.
Commercialization depends on something broader.
Quantum technology sits at the intersection of physics, mathematics, engineering, computer science, manufacturing, cybersecurity, and industry-specific expertise. No single organization possesses every capability required to transform research into operational value.
Universities drive foundational discovery.
Startups accelerate product development.
Industry partners provide practical use cases.
Government and defense organizations help validate technologies under real-world constraints.
Each participant contributes a different piece of the commercialization puzzle.
When these groups work independently, progress slows. When they collaborate, innovation becomes significantly more practical and scalable.
## Why Industry Adoption Depends on Integration
One of the most common misconceptions about quantum computing is that adoption will require organizations to abandon existing systems and workflows.
The reality is likely to be very different.
Most enterprises have spent decades building engineering, simulation, and computational workflows around classical computing environments. They are not looking to replace those systems overnight.
They are looking to enhance them.
The future of quantum adoption will likely be hybrid. Quantum technologies will augment existing high-performance computing infrastructure, enabling engineers and scientists to solve problems that remain inaccessible to classical approaches alone.
Success will depend less on building standalone quantum environments and more on integrating quantum capabilities into tools industries already trust and use.
The easier that integration becomes, the faster commercialization will occur.
## Why Defense Organizations Play a Critical Role
Defense organizations have historically served as early adopters of transformative technologies.
Quantum is following a similar trajectory.
Organizations such as AFRL Rome provide access to mission-critical challenges involving optimization, simulation, secure communications, and advanced computational analysis. These environments offer something every emerging technology needs: validation.
For startups, validation is often more valuable than funding alone.
It demonstrates that a technology can move beyond theoretical potential and deliver measurable results under demanding operational conditions.
Defense partnerships also strengthen broader ecosystems by connecting researchers, startups, industry partners, and government stakeholders around shared objectives.
The result is faster innovation and clearer pathways to deployment.
## Building the Infrastructure for Long-Term Growth
Commercializing quantum technology requires more than scientific excellence.
It requires talent pipelines, testing environments, computing infrastructure, funding mechanisms, and collaborative frameworks capable of supporting long development cycles.
Regional investment initiatives help create that foundation.
They reduce barriers between academia, industry, and government while attracting additional private investment and technical talent.
Just as importantly, they provide confidence.
Investors are more willing to commit capital. Enterprises become more willing to explore adoption. Startups gain access to resources that would otherwise be difficult to obtain.
Ecosystems create momentum. Momentum accelerates commercialization.
## Quantum Will Be Built by Ecosystems
The next phase of quantum innovation will not be defined solely by who builds the best hardware or develops the most sophisticated algorithms.
It will be defined by who creates the most effective pathways from discovery to deployment.
Regions capable of connecting research, industry, government, and startups into a unified innovation engine will have a significant advantage.
What is happening across Upstate New York offers a glimpse of that future.
The ecosystem emerging there is not centered around a single company or institution. It is being built through collaboration across sectors, disciplines, and organizations.
That model may ultimately become one of the most important innovations in quantum commercialization itself.
Because the future of quantum will not be built by isolated breakthroughs.
It will be built by ecosystems capable of turning breakthroughs into impact.

## European Quantum Ecosystems Shift Focus from Research to Industrialization
**Author:** [John Moore](https://jmwriter4.substack.com/p/european-ecosystems-highlight-research)  
**Published:** May 13, 2026
Europe's quantum ecosystem is entering a new phase, transitioning from research excellence toward industrial-scale deployment and commercialization.
In Part 1 of a three-part analysis of quantum technology hubs across Europe and the United Kingdom, John Moore examines 18 quantum ecosystems and highlights the institutions, partnerships, and policy initiatives shaping the region's quantum future.
The report notes that Europe maintains a strong position in global quantum research, supported by leading universities, research institutes, and national laboratories. According to Elsevier's 2025 analysis, the European Union accounts for approximately 26% of global quantum research output, second only to China.
Many of Europe's leading ecosystems are anchored by universities and research organizations, including the University of Oxford, University of Cambridge, Sorbonne Université, Université Grenoble Alpes, CNRS, Inria, Max Planck Institutes, VTT, and TNO. These institutions continue to play a central role in advancing quantum science while supporting emerging commercialization efforts.
A key theme of the article is Europe's challenge of converting scientific leadership into scalable industrial capability. While the region has established a strong research foundation, policymakers and industry leaders are increasingly focused on accelerating the transition from laboratory research to deployable quantum infrastructure.
The article highlights initiatives such as the European Commission's Quantum Europe Strategy and the proposed Quantum Act, both designed to strengthen Europe's position in quantum computing, quantum communications, and quantum sensing while supporting ecosystem growth and industrial adoption.
Commercial momentum is also increasing through partnerships between research institutions and industry. Examples cited include IonQ's planned Quantum Innovation Centre in Cambridge and quantum hardware developments involving organizations such as ORCA Computing, Oxford Quantum Circuits, Quobly, and PsiQuantum.
The analysis concludes that access to capital, ecosystem coordination, industry partnerships, and regulatory frameworks will play a critical role in determining how effectively Europe converts its research strengths into commercial quantum leadership.
**Original Article:** [_European ecosystems highlight research, gear up for industrialization_](https://jmwriter4.substack.com/p/european-ecosystems-highlight-research) by John Moore, published in _Tech Ecosystem Explorer_ on May 13, 2026.

## Aerospace America Features BQP Founder’s Journey from Student Engineer to Aerospace Innovator
Aerospace America, the flagship publication of the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics (AIAA), recently featured BQP Co-Founder and CTO Rut Lineswala in an article exploring how sustained engagement with AIAA helped shape his journey from engineering student to entrepreneur.
The feature traces Lineswala’s involvement with AIAA beginning in 2015 as a freshman at Rutgers University, where participation in the Design/Build/Fly competition introduced him to practical engineering problem-solving beyond the classroom. The article highlights how experiences gained through student competitions, industry networking, internships, and participation in AIAA SciTech Forum contributed to the development of the engineering mindset that would later influence the founding of BQP.
The article also discusses the origins of BQP during the COVID-19 pandemic and the company’s mission to address computational bottlenecks in aerospace engineering. According to the feature, BQP is focused on accelerating engineering simulation and optimization workflows through quantum-enhanced computing, physics-informed machine learning, and advanced computational methods that integrate with existing engineering environments.
As BQP expanded its presence within the aerospace ecosystem, the company became an AIAA Corporate Member and increased its engagement through technical publications, conference participation, research collaborations, and industry events. The article notes that BQP regularly contributes technical papers at AIAA conferences and works closely with academic and industry partners to advance next-generation engineering technologies.
A key theme throughout the feature is the role of professional communities in supporting innovation across an engineer’s career—from education and research to entrepreneurship and industry leadership.
The story highlights how organizations such as AIAA can provide a continuous platform for learning, collaboration, technical exchange, and industry engagement throughout the aerospace innovation lifecycle.

## The Data Center Efficiency Crisis Nobody Is Talking About
Originally published on [Built In  
](https://embeddedscience.org/2026/04/01/data-center-efficiency-crisis-quantum/)Reviewed by Seth Wilson | Jan. 06, 2026
**By Abhishek Chopra**  
Published on Jan. 07, 2026
For decades, defense capability has been defined by physical scale—jets, tanks, ships and missile systems. Strategic advantage was measured in inventory, range and firepower.
That model is no longer sufficient. In a threat environment shaped by rapid technological iteration, the decisive factor is not what can be built, but how quickly systems can evolve. Air superiority is no longer determined by tonnage or fleet size. It is determined by software velocity, system architecture and the ability to update mission logic in near real time.
This shift is structural. It forces a reconsideration of how defense programs are architected, how readiness is measured and how capital is allocated. A software-first approach is not a tooling upgrade—it is a change in operating model.
## What Is Software-Defined Defense?
**Iteration Speed:** Capability is measured by how quickly mission logic, control systems and algorithms can be updated and deployed, rather than by platform output.
**Virtual Prototyping:** Simulation-driven environments enable systems to be validated and optimized before physical production, reducing cost and late-stage risk.
**Operational Readiness:** Continuous delivery models such as DevSecOps replace static upgrade cycles, ensuring systems remain adaptable in dynamic environments.
## Why the Shift From Metal to Code Matters
According to McKinsey & Company, scaling AI, advanced simulation and quantum computing could unlock a $250 billion opportunity in defense modernization. The implication is direct: hardware-centric planning is increasingly misaligned with operational needs.
Engineering workflows are already shifting. Simulation environments now allow systems to be designed, stress-tested and optimized before manufacturing begins. Virtual prototypes can evaluate performance, identify constraints and refine mission logic without incurring physical costs.
In parallel, adversaries are accelerating their own development cycles. In this context, speed becomes the defining variable. The ability to iterate rapidly—deploying updates in weeks rather than years—translates directly into operational advantage.
## What Better Code Actually Unlocks
The transition to software-defined systems fundamentally changes how capability is developed and sustained.
Iteration speed becomes the primary metric. Progress is no longer tied to the number of platforms produced, but to how quickly systems can be updated, validated and redeployed. Capability evolves continuously rather than through periodic hardware refresh cycles.
When software delivery slows, readiness degrades. Analysis from the U.S. Government Accountability Office shows that programs such as the F-35 Joint Strike Fighter Program are increasingly constrained by software maturity rather than production output. Modernization efforts depend on stable and timely software upgrades to unlock functionality across deployed systems. Delays in software integration can leave fully built platforms operationally limited.
This represents a shift in upgrade economics. Hardware improvements require extended procurement cycles, factory capacity and capital investment. Software-driven enhancements can be deployed across existing systems without restarting production pipelines. As a result, platform lifespan and operational value are increasingly determined by software.
This dependency also improves resilience. In environments affected by supply chain constraints and long hardware lead times, software-centric systems provide flexibility. Mission logic can be updated, tested in simulation and deployed without waiting for physical components.
Programs such as Kessel Run illustrate how reorganizing around software delivery can compress iteration cycles. By adopting DevSecOps practices and aligning incentives around continuous deployment, these models enable capability updates on significantly shorter timelines.
However, scaling this approach introduces systemic challenges. While technological capability exists, adoption is often constrained by fragmented funding, slow acquisition pathways, legacy infrastructure and limited compute availability at the tactical edge. Without changes to delivery models, even mature technologies risk underutilization.
## Why Legacy Investment Models Are Misaligned
Many defense investment frameworks remain structured around hardware delivery. Budgeting and procurement processes continue to prioritize physical systems, with software and digital infrastructure treated as secondary considerations.
This creates a mismatch between development cycles. Hardware operates on multi-year timelines, while software evolves continuously. Applying legacy acquisition models to software-defined systems introduces delays and reduces responsiveness.
Cultural factors further reinforce this misalignment. Software teams are often positioned as support functions rather than core contributors to mission capability. This limits integration across engineering, operations and decision-making layers.
Measurement frameworks have also not adapted. Readiness is still frequently tracked using platform counts or asset availability, rather than indicators such as update velocity, system uptime and software maturity.
## What Tech Leaders in Defense Should Do Now
The transition begins with architecture. Platforms should be designed as modular systems where mission software is decoupled from hardware. This enables continuous updates and reduces dependency on physical redesign cycles.
Investment in simulation and virtual prototyping is critical. Digital environments allow teams to test scenarios, identify constraints and refine systems before manufacturing. This reduces downstream risk and accelerates development timelines.
Measurement systems need recalibration. Organizations should track iteration speed, deployment latency and real-time performance rather than relying solely on production metrics. These indicators provide a more accurate representation of operational readiness.
Procurement and supplier models must also evolve. Contracts should incentivize continuous delivery, integration speed and system adaptability rather than one-time production milestones. This shifts the focus toward sustained capability development.
## A Cultural Shift and a Technical One
Adopting a software-first approach requires more than technical investment. It demands a shift in how defense organizations define capability and manage systems. Platforms must be treated as evolving ecosystems rather than static assets.
This requires alignment between engineering, software and operational teams, supported by governance models that enable continuous iteration. Without this alignment, technical improvements will remain constrained by organizational structures.
Physical systems alone no longer define readiness. If the software governing them is outdated, operational effectiveness is inherently limited. As emerging technologies scale, the ability to adapt quickly becomes the primary determinant of strategic advantage.
The next breakthrough will not be defined solely by manufacturing capacity. It will emerge from faster algorithms, simulation-driven design and continuous software evolution.
If capability is now determined in code, continuing to prioritize metal in budgets and strategy reflects a structural lag. The conditions defining defense innovation have already shifted. The operating model must adapt accordingly.

When software delivery lags, readiness suffers. According to the U.S. Government Accountability Office’s (GAO) 2024 assessment of the F-35 program, aircraft availability and mission capability are now constrained less by production rates and more by the maturity and stability of onboard software. Modernization efforts such as Block 4 rely primarily on software upgrades to unlock new functionality across the existing fleet. GAO documents that delays or instability in software delivery can leave aircraft delivered but operationally limited, demonstrating how decisively software execution now shapes readiness.
This represents a structural shift in how capability is upgraded. Hardware refreshes require new factory capacity, long procurement cycles and significant capital investment. Software-driven upgrades, by contrast, allow new mission functionality, system improvements and performance gains to be applied across existing platforms without restarting production lines. As defense systems become more software-defined, platform lifespan and operational value increasingly depend on code rather than metal.
That dependence also reshapes strategic resilience. In an environment of strained global supply chains and extended hardware lead times, systems that evolve primarily through software offer greater flexibility than those reliant on physical components. The ability to adapt mission logic, integrate new capabilities and validate changes through simulation allows defense forces to adjust faster than procurement timelines alone would permit.
There are already signs of what this shift from hardware to software readiness looks like in practice. The U.S. Air Force’s Kessel Run program demonstrated how reorganizing around software delivery can dramatically compress iteration cycles. By adopting DevSecOps practices and treating software as mission-critical infrastructure, Kessel Run demonstrated that software updates for operational systems no longer had to wait on multi-year acquisition cycles, with teams delivering usable capability on quarterly timelines instead of four- to five-year release schedules. When delivery models, incentives and authority align around software velocity, code becomes a capability accelerator rather than a bottleneck.
McKinsey’s research reinforces this. The firm estimates a $250 billion opportunity in modernizing defense by scaling technologies such as AI, advanced simulation and quantum computing but warns that capital alone is not enough. Across defense ecosystems, the biggest bottlenecks are no longer invention, but adoption: fragmented funding, slow acquisition pathways, legacy infrastructure and limited computing capacity at the tactical edge.
Without new delivery models that prioritize software-defined systems, modular upgrades and faster transition from prototype to fielded capability, even mature technologies risk stalling before they produce operational impact.
## Why Legacy Investment Models Are Misaligned
Despite all this, many defense budgets remain stuck in a 20th-century mindset. GAO assessments of major defense programs like the F-35 Joint Strike Fighter Program show that acquisition and budgeting processes remain structured around hardware delivery, with software, digital infrastructure and simulation treated as secondary investments rather than primary capability drivers.
As former Kessel Run leaders have argued, the DoD continues to apply Cold War-era acquisition models to software-defined warfare. We are still structuring budgets and procurement processes around hardware timelines rather than sustained investment in digital infrastructure and continuous delivery.
That’s a problem because hardware delivery runs on multi-year timelines, while software moves in sprints. When organizations treat code as an afterthought, they give up the agility that defines modern defense readiness.
There’s also a cultural challenge. Many organizations still see software teams as “support,” rather than as mission-critical contributors. That mindset slows innovation and discourages cross-functional collaboration.
And metrics haven’t caught up either. Readiness is still tracked in platform counts or tonnage rather than update velocity, availability or system maturity.
## What Tech Leaders in Defense Should Do Now
For defense technology leaders including CTOs, engineering VPs, software architects and program managers, this shift starts with reframing the architecture. Treat your platforms as living systems. Map where simulation, software and mission-logic updates can drive more value than another round of procurement. Build modular systems that separate mission software from the hardware shell, so over-the-air updates and rapid improvements become possible.
The next step is to invest in simulation and virtual prototyping. The ability to “fail fast” in simulation is what separates the organizations that innovate continuously from those that stagnate. Digital twins and design-space exploration tools help teams test scenarios, catch flaws and make changes before the first component is ever built. Every digital failure saves time, money and human risk later on.
Measurement needs a reboot, too. Old KPIs like “jets delivered” or “hardware cost” don’t tell the story anymore. Start tracking iteration speed, update frequency and uptime. How many aircraft are running the latest build? How long does it take to push a patch to the field? These are the metrics that define digital-age readiness.
Finally, funding and supplier relationships have to evolve. Partners should be incentivized for continuous delivery, faster software cycles, sensor integrations and mission-logic refreshes, not just production volume. Shift contracts from “build once” to “iterate continuously.” In practice, that means paying for agility, not just assembly.
## A Cultural Shift and a Technical One
Becoming software-first isn’t just about writing more code. It’s about changing how defense leaders think about innovation itself. Platforms can’t be treated as finished products; they must be managed as evolving ecosystems. That requires tight collaboration between design, software and operations from day one.
Flight hours and physical assets don’t tell the full story anymore. If the software running those systems is outdated, readiness is only an illusion. McKinsey’s data shows the window to modernize is narrowing as emerging technologies like AI and quantum simulation scale globally. The defense organizations that move fastest will define the next era of readiness.
Because the next breakthrough won’t roll off an assembly line. It’ll come from the cloud. It’ll come from better algorithms, faster updates and simulation-driven design. If air superiority is decided in code first, why is the budget still built for metal? The mission has changed, and so must the mindset.

## BQP Unveils Hybrid Quantum-Classical Approach for CFD Simulations in Aerospace
Originally published on [HPCwire](https://www.hpcwire.com/off-the-wire/bqp-unveils-hybrid-quantum-classical-approach-for-cfd-simulations-in-aerospace/)
**By HPCwire, NEW YORK, Sept. 12, 2024** — [BQP](https://www.bqpsim.com/), a startup leading the development of quantum-based engineering simulations, today announced a significant research milestone for simulating Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). The milestone was achieved using a hybrid quantum-classical solver which is part of BQP’s next-gen simulation platform, BQPhy.
After conducting ~100,000 experiments, BQP researchers published their work in a [paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.03241) where they estimated that large-scale CFD simulation of a jet engine can be achieved with only 30 logical qubits on a quantum computer, leading to better accuracy, efficiency, and costs than current methods. A prior study, which inspired the BQP team to undertake this research, found that it required 19.2M compute cores to perform this same simulation with classical algorithms on state-of-the-art High-Performance Computers (HPCs).
> “This study is pivotal as it would democratize large-scale CFD simulation for every engineer once quantum computers become utility-scale,” said Abhishek Chopra, founder, CEO, and Chief Scientific Officer at BQP. “In the future, what would engineers have easier access to – 19.2M HPC cores or 30-logical-qubit quantum computers? I bet on the latter.”
_“With continued research, we believe that quantum computing has the potential to revolutionize the way simulations are conducted, allowing engineers to push the boundaries of design and engineering,”_ Chopra added.
> “BQP’s results signal the introduction of drastically higher computing power to flow field analysis and simulation. This capability can unlock new methods in aerospace development, enabling higher confidence during design and more proactive maintenance during the aircraft life cycle,” said Dan Hart, Senior Aerospace Executive and Member of the National Academy of Engineering.
For the research, BQP scientists estimated scalability, accuracy, and consistency for jet engine simulations using BQP’s Hybrid Quantum Classical Finite Method (HQCFM) solver. The study demonstrated the scalability of the HQCFM solver, by simulating a non-linear time-dependent Partial Differential Equation (PDE) from 4 to all the way to 11 qubits.
Researchers found that accuracy and consistency were comparable to classical computers, while the HQCFM distinguished itself by running inside a time loop in a transient problem, without propagating any error to the next time step. Obtaining such high accuracy consistently is a significant breakthrough toward more complex simulations beyond the capacity of classical devices.
BQP believes that BQPhy’s solver will allow CFD engineers to simulate a full aircraft for the first time, allowing aerospace engineers to greatly improve flight patterns during turbulence. Given current trends in supercomputing computational advances, simulating an entire aircraft via classical computing would not be possible until 2080.
In fact, BQPhy’s physics-based solver can also be used to solve other PDEs to capture interactions in gas dynamics, traffic flow or flood waves in rivers. Combined with quantum algorithms, the technology can accurately solve complex equations with reduced hardware demands compared to traditional high-performance computing (HPC) methods, while enabling sophisticated and intricate simulations to be performed effectively.
“Building on our successful collaborations with leading academic institutions, government research agencies such as AFRL, DARPA, industry pioneers, and top academic institutes, BQP is eager to partner with organizations that share our vision for advancing quantum computing solutions,” said Chopra.

## BQP Reports on Aircraft CFD Simulations with Quantum Computing
Originally published by [InsideHPC](https://insidehpc.com/2024/09/bqp-reports-on-aircraft-cfd-simulations-with-quantum-computing/)
BQP, a startup developing quantum-based engineering simulations, today announced what it said is a research milestone for simulating computational fluid dynamics. The milestone was achieved using a hybrid quantum-classical solver which is part of BQP’s next-gen simulation platform, BQPhy®, according to BQP.
After conducting ~100000 experiments, BQP researchers published their work in a [paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.03241) where they estimated that large-scale CFD simulation of a jet engine can be achieved with only 30 logical qubits on a quantum computer, leading to better accuracy, efficiency, and costs than current methods. A prior [study](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3581784.3627040), which inspired the BQP team to undertake this research, found that it required 19.2M compute cores to perform this same simulation with classical algorithms on high-performance computers.
“This study is pivotal as it would democratize large-scale CFD simulation for every engineer once quantum computers become utility-scale,” said Abhishek Chopra, founder, CEO and chief scientific officer at BQP. “In the future, what would engineers have easier access to – 19.2M HPC cores or 30-logical-qubit quantum computers? I bet on the latter.”
“With continued research, we believe that quantum computing has the potential to revolutionize the way simulations are conducted, allowing engineers to push the boundaries of design and engineering,” Chopra added.
“BQP’s results signal the introduction of drastically higher computing power to flow field analysis and simulation. This capability can unlock new methods in aerospace development, enabling higher confidence during design and more proactive maintenance during the aircraft life cycle,” said Dan Hart, Senior Aerospace Executive and Member of the National Academy of Engineering.
For the research, BQP scientists estimated scalability, accuracy, and consistency for jet engine simulations using BQP’s Hybrid Quantum Classical Finite Method (HQCFM) solver. The study demonstrated the scalability of the HQCFM solver, by simulating a non-linear time-dependent Partial Differential Equation (PDE) from 4 to all the way to 11 qubits.
Researchers found that accuracy and consistency were comparable to classical computers, while the HQCFM distinguished itself by running inside a time loop in a transient problem, without propagating any error to the next time step. Obtaining such high accuracy consistently is a significant breakthrough toward more complex simulations beyond the capacity of classical devices.
BQP believes that BQPhy®’s solver will allow CFD engineers to simulate a full aircraft for the first time, allowing aerospace engineers to greatly improve flight patterns during turbulence. Given current trends in supercomputing computational advances, simulating an entire aircraft via classical computing would not be [possible](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/10.1098/rsta.2013.0320) until 2080.
In fact, BQPhy®’s physics-based solver can also be used to solve other PDEs to capture interactions in gas dynamics, traffic flow or flood waves in rivers. Combined with quantum algorithms, the technology can accurately solve complex equations with reduced hardware demands compared to traditional high-performance computing (HPC) methods, while enabling sophisticated and intricate simulations to be performed effectively.
“Building on our successful collaborations with leading academic institutions, government research agencies such as AFRL, DARPA, industry pioneers, and top academic institutes, BQP is eager to partner with organizations that share our vision for advancing quantum computing solutions,” said Chopra.
An extension of the study has been accepted for the [the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics](https://www.aiaa.org/SciTech) SciTech Forum in January 2025, the world’s largest aerospace research, development, and technology event.

BQP Appoints NUAIR CEO Craig Marcinkowski to Board as U.S. Defense and Aerospace Growth Accelerates

‍

SYRACUSE, N.Y. — June 12, 2026

Originally published by: Citybiz.co

BQP, a leader in quantum-inspired simulation and digital twin technologies for mission-critical engineering applications, today announced the appointment of aerospace and defense technology executive Craig Marcinkowski to its Board of Directors.

The appointment comes as BQP expands its engagement across the U.S. aerospace, defense, and advanced manufacturing sectors, driven by increasing demand for computational technologies that accelerate engineering design, optimization, and mission planning.

Marcinkowski currently serves as President and Chief Executive Officer of NUAIR, a nationally recognized organization advancing uncrewed systems, advanced air mobility, and aerospace innovation. Over the course of his career, he has held leadership roles at SRC Inc., Lockheed Martin, Saab Inc., and Fotokite, helping organizations develop, commercialize, and deploy advanced technologies across defense, surveillance, aerospace, and public safety markets.

His addition to the Board strengthens BQP's strategic leadership as the company scales deployments of its quantum-inspired engineering platform across industries where simulation performance, computational efficiency, and digital engineering capabilities are becoming increasingly critical.

Supporting the Next Phase of Growth

BQP has experienced significant growth over the past year as organizations seek new approaches to address increasingly complex engineering and optimization challenges. The company is currently collaborating with the U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory and working alongside technology leaders including NVIDIA, MathWorks, IBM, Intel, as well as several Tier I aerospace and automotive organizations.

As engineering teams face growing computational demands across areas such as computational fluid dynamics, design optimization, mission planning, and machine learning, BQP's technology enables organizations to accelerate simulation and decision-making using today's computing infrastructure.

“BQP is operating at the intersection of several major technology transitions affecting engineering and national security,” said Marcinkowski. “Organizations are looking for ways to model, optimize, and validate increasingly complex systems without waiting for future hardware breakthroughs. BQP has developed an approach that delivers practical value today while positioning customers for the future of advanced computing. I’m excited to support the company during this next stage of growth.”

Advancing Quantum-Inspired Engineering

Founded with the vision of bringing quantum-inspired computing into real-world engineering environments, BQP develops technologies that help organizations solve computationally intensive problems using existing CPU, GPU, and HPC infrastructure.

Its flagship platform, BQPhy®, combines quantum-inspired optimization, simulation, and digital twin capabilities to address bottlenecks in engineering workflows. The platform integrates with existing software environments and allows organizations to improve performance without disrupting established processes or waiting for large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computers.

“Craig brings deep expertise across aerospace, defense, advanced systems development, and technology commercialization,” said Abhishek Chopra, Founder, Chief Executive Officer, and Chief Scientific Officer of BQP. “His experience building organizations and guiding innovation in highly regulated and mission-critical industries will be invaluable as we continue expanding our presence across the U.S. market. We are pleased to welcome him to the Board during this important phase of growth.”

Strengthening U.S. Aerospace and Defense Engagement

The appointment reflects BQP's continued investment in the aerospace and defense ecosystem, where digital engineering, simulation-driven design, and advanced optimization are becoming essential capabilities for maintaining technological advantage.

As governments and industry accelerate adoption of digital twins, autonomous systems, and AI-enabled engineering workflows, BQP is positioning its quantum-inspired technologies to help organizations solve increasingly complex challenges while leveraging existing computational infrastructure.

With Marcinkowski joining the Board, BQP further strengthens its leadership team as it expands collaborations with government agencies, research institutions, and industry partners focused on next-generation aerospace and defense innovation.

How Regional Quantum Hubs Could Accelerate Commercialization in the US

By Louis Castel,
Published on June 11, 2026

Originally published on AZoQuantum

For years, the quantum industry has focused on a single question: When will the technology be ready?

A more important question is now emerging: When will the ecosystem be ready?

The challenge facing quantum technology is no longer limited to scientific breakthroughs. Across the industry, researchers are demonstrating new advances in quantum hardware, algorithms, networking, and simulation. Yet commercialization remains the defining bottleneck.

History shows that transformative technologies rarely succeed because of a single invention. They succeed because ecosystems emerge around them. Semiconductors required manufacturing clusters. Aerospace required supply chains and testing infrastructure. Artificial intelligence required cloud computing and developer communities.

Quantum will be no different.

The regions that successfully connect research institutions, startups, industry partners, government agencies, and investors will likely determine which innovations move beyond the laboratory and into real-world deployment.

That is why initiatives such as the Advancing Regional Quantum Hubs Act represent more than economic development programs. They represent a shift in how quantum commercialization is being approached in the United States.

Why Geography Still Matters in Deep Technology

The digital economy has created the perception that innovation can happen anywhere.

In reality, deep technology industries still depend heavily on physical ecosystems.

Quantum development requires access to specialized talent, advanced computing resources, laboratory infrastructure, manufacturing expertise, and application-focused partnerships. Building these capabilities independently is difficult. Building them collectively accelerates progress.

Regional hubs create environments where researchers, engineers, startups, enterprise partners, and government organizations can work on common challenges while shortening the distance between discovery and deployment.

The value is not simply proximity. It is connectivity.

The faster ideas move between research, validation, commercialization, and adoption, the faster an industry matures.

Why Upstate New York Is Emerging as a Quantum Powerhouse

Few regions illustrate this model more effectively than Upstate New York.

The region already possesses many of the ingredients required for quantum commercialization: advanced manufacturing capabilities, semiconductor expertise, defense infrastructure, high-performance computing resources, and world-class universities.

Equally important is the growing network connecting these capabilities.

Across the New York Thruway corridor, startups, research institutions, defense organizations, and enterprise technology companies are building an increasingly collaborative ecosystem. Instead of operating as isolated stakeholders, these organizations are contributing to a shared vision for quantum innovation.

That collaborative model may become one of the region's greatest competitive advantages.

Innovation accelerates when expertise flows freely across organizational boundaries.

Commercialization Is a Collaboration Problem

Many discussions about quantum focus on hardware performance, qubit counts, or algorithmic breakthroughs.

Commercialization depends on something broader.

Quantum technology sits at the intersection of physics, mathematics, engineering, computer science, manufacturing, cybersecurity, and industry-specific expertise. No single organization possesses every capability required to transform research into operational value.

Universities drive foundational discovery.

Startups accelerate product development.

Industry partners provide practical use cases.

Government and defense organizations help validate technologies under real-world constraints.

Each participant contributes a different piece of the commercialization puzzle.

When these groups work independently, progress slows. When they collaborate, innovation becomes significantly more practical and scalable.

Why Industry Adoption Depends on Integration

One of the most common misconceptions about quantum computing is that adoption will require organizations to abandon existing systems and workflows.

The reality is likely to be very different.

Most enterprises have spent decades building engineering, simulation, and computational workflows around classical computing environments. They are not looking to replace those systems overnight.

They are looking to enhance them.

The future of quantum adoption will likely be hybrid. Quantum technologies will augment existing high-performance computing infrastructure, enabling engineers and scientists to solve problems that remain inaccessible to classical approaches alone.

The Data Center Efficiency Crisis Nobody Is Talking About

Originally published on Built In
Reviewed by Seth Wilson | Jan. 06, 2026

By Abhishek Chopra
Published on Jan. 07, 2026

For decades, defense capability has been defined by physical scale—jets, tanks, ships and missile systems. Strategic advantage was measured in inventory, range and firepower.

That model is no longer sufficient. In a threat environment shaped by rapid technological iteration, the decisive factor is not what can be built, but how quickly systems can evolve. Air superiority is no longer determined by tonnage or fleet size. It is determined by software velocity, system architecture and the ability to update mission logic in near real time.

This shift is structural. It forces a reconsideration of how defense programs are architected, how readiness is measured and how capital is allocated. A software-first approach is not a tooling upgrade—it is a change in operating model.

What Is Software-Defined Defense?

Iteration Speed: Capability is measured by how quickly mission logic, control systems and algorithms can be updated and deployed, rather than by platform output.

Virtual Prototyping: Simulation-driven environments enable systems to be validated and optimized before physical production, reducing cost and late-stage risk.

Operational Readiness: Continuous delivery models such as DevSecOps replace static upgrade cycles, ensuring systems remain adaptable in dynamic environments.

Why the Shift From Metal to Code Matters

According to McKinsey & Company, scaling AI, advanced simulation and quantum computing could unlock a $250 billion opportunity in defense modernization. The implication is direct: hardware-centric planning is increasingly misaligned with operational needs.

Engineering workflows are already shifting. Simulation environments now allow systems to be designed, stress-tested and optimized before manufacturing begins. Virtual prototypes can evaluate performance, identify constraints and refine mission logic without incurring physical costs.

In parallel, adversaries are accelerating their own development cycles. In this context, speed becomes the defining variable. The ability to iterate rapidly—deploying updates in weeks rather than years—translates directly into operational advantage.

What Better Code Actually Unlocks

The transition to software-defined systems fundamentally changes how capability is developed and sustained.

Iteration speed becomes the primary metric. Progress is no longer tied to the number of platforms produced, but to how quickly systems can be updated, validated and redeployed. Capability evolves continuously rather than through periodic hardware refresh cycles.

When software delivery slows, readiness degrades. Analysis from the U.S. Government Accountability Office shows that programs such as the F-35 Joint Strike Fighter Program are increasingly constrained by software maturity rather than production output. Modernization efforts depend on stable and timely software upgrades to unlock functionality across deployed systems. Delays in software integration can leave fully built platforms operationally limited.

This represents a shift in upgrade economics. Hardware improvements require extended procurement cycles, factory capacity and capital investment. Software-driven enhancements can be deployed across existing systems without restarting production pipelines. As a result, platform lifespan and operational value are increasingly determined by software.

This dependency also improves resilience. In environments affected by supply chain constraints and long hardware lead times, software-centric systems provide flexibility. Mission logic can be updated, tested in simulation and deployed without waiting for physical components.

Programs such as Kessel Run illustrate how reorganizing around software delivery can compress iteration cycles. By adopting DevSecOps practices and aligning incentives around continuous deployment, these models enable capability updates on significantly shorter timelines.

However, scaling this approach introduces systemic challenges. While technological capability exists, adoption is often constrained by fragmented funding, slow acquisition pathways, legacy infrastructure and limited compute availability at the tactical edge. Without changes to delivery models, even mature technologies risk underutilization.

Why Legacy Investment Models Are Misaligned

Many defense investment frameworks remain structured around hardware delivery. Budgeting and procurement processes continue to prioritize physical systems, with software and digital infrastructure treated as secondary considerations.

This creates a mismatch between development cycles. Hardware operates on multi-year timelines, while software evolves continuously. Applying legacy acquisition models to software-defined systems introduces delays and reduces responsiveness.

Cultural factors further reinforce this misalignment. Software teams are often positioned as support functions rather than core contributors to mission capability. This limits integration across engineering, operations and decision-making layers.

Measurement frameworks have also not adapted. Readiness is still frequently tracked using platform counts or asset availability, rather than indicators such as update velocity, system uptime and software maturity.

What Tech Leaders in Defense Should Do Now

The transition begins with architecture. Platforms should be designed as modular systems where mission software is decoupled from hardware. This enables continuous updates and reduces dependency on physical redesign cycles.

Investment in simulation and virtual prototyping is critical. Digital environments allow teams to test scenarios, identify constraints and refine systems before manufacturing. This reduces downstream risk and accelerates development timelines.

Measurement systems need recalibration. Organizations should track iteration speed, deployment latency and real-time performance rather than relying solely on production metrics. These indicators provide a more accurate representation of operational readiness.

Procurement and supplier models must also evolve. Contracts should incentivize continuous delivery, integration speed and system adaptability rather than one-time production milestones. This shifts the focus toward sustained capability development.

A Cultural Shift and a Technical One

Adopting a software-first approach requires more than technical investment. It demands a shift in how defense organizations define capability and manage systems. Platforms must be treated as evolving ecosystems rather than static assets.

This requires alignment between engineering, software and operational teams, supported by governance models that enable continuous iteration. Without this alignment, technical improvements will remain constrained by organizational structures.

Physical systems alone no longer define readiness. If the software governing them is outdated, operational effectiveness is inherently limited. As emerging technologies scale, the ability to adapt quickly becomes the primary determinant of strategic advantage.

The next breakthrough will not be defined solely by manufacturing capacity. It will emerge from faster algorithms, simulation-driven design and continuous software evolution.

If capability is now determined in code, continuing to prioritize metal in budgets and strategy reflects a structural lag. The conditions defining defense innovation have already shifted. The operating model must adapt accordingly.

Why the Next Arms Race Will Be Won in Code, Not Metal

Originally published on Built In
Reviewed by Seth Wilson | Jan 06, 2026

By Abhishek Chopra
Published on Jan. 07, 2026

For decades, defense budgets have flowed into platforms like jets, tanks, ships and missiles. But in today’s rapidly changing threat environment, the real edge lies not in what you can build, but in how fast you can improve it. Supremacy in the skies is no longer measured in raw tonnage. It’s measured in iteration speed and software architecture.

This shift forces a hard reconsideration of how defense programs are structured, how readiness is measured and how funding gets allocated. A software-first approach isn’t just a technical upgrade. It’s a cultural transformation.

What Is Software-Defined Defense?

Iteration Speed: Measuring success by how fast mission logic and algorithms are updated rather than platform counts.

Virtual Prototyping: Using AI and simulation to test systems before physical production to reduce costs and risk.

Operational Readiness: Moving away from “metal” constraints toward continuous delivery cycles (DevSecOps) to maintain a strategic edge.

Why the Shift From Metal to Code Matters

McKinsey estimates there is a $250 billion opportunity for defense organizations that scale emerging technologies like AI, advanced simulation and quantum computing. That figure alone should be a wake-up call: Hardware-only planning is already outdated.

Software and simulation tools are rewriting how engineers build and test complex systems. Virtual prototypes can “fly” before they ever take shape in the real world. Engineers can stress-test components, optimize mission logic and validate performance long before manufacturing begins. The result? Lower costs, faster deployment and fewer surprises in the field.

In an era where adversaries iterate quickly, that speed is everything. A nation that can update its defense systems in weeks, not years, will always have the upper hand.

What Better Code Actually Unlocks

Shifting from a hardware-centric model to a software-driven one changes how progress is measured and exposes where current defense systems fall short.

Iteration time becomes the new crucial metric for success. In a software-defined defense ecosystem, progress is no longer measured by the number of platforms delivered but by how quickly mission software, algorithms and system logic can be updated and tested. Capability advances through continuous iteration rather than episodic hardware refreshes.

When software delivery lags, readiness suffers. According to the U.S. Government Accountability Office’s (GAO) 2024 assessment of the F-35 program, aircraft availability and mission capability are now constrained less by production rates and more by the maturity and stability of onboard software. Modernization efforts such as Block 4 rely primarily on software upgrades to unlock new functionality across the existing fleet. GAO documents that delays or instability in software delivery can leave aircraft delivered but operationally limited, demonstrating how decisively software execution now shapes readiness.

This represents a structural shift in how capability is upgraded. Hardware refreshes require new factory capacity, long procurement cycles and significant capital investment. Software-driven upgrades, by contrast, allow new mission functionality, system improvements and performance gains to be applied across existing platforms without restarting production lines. As defense systems become more software-defined, platform lifespan and operational value increasingly depend on code rather than metal.

That dependence also reshapes strategic resilience. In an environment of strained global supply chains and extended hardware lead times, systems that evolve primarily through software offer greater flexibility than those reliant on physical components. The ability to adapt mission logic, integrate new capabilities and validate changes through simulation allows defense forces to adjust faster than procurement timelines alone would permit.

There are already signs of what this shift from hardware to software readiness looks like in practice. The U.S. Air Force’s Kessel Run program demonstrated how reorganizing around software delivery can dramatically compress iteration cycles. By adopting DevSecOps practices and treating software as mission-critical infrastructure, Kessel Run demonstrated that software updates for operational systems no longer had to wait on multi-year acquisition cycles, with teams delivering usable capability on quarterly timelines instead of four- to five-year release schedules. When delivery models, incentives and authority align around software velocity, code becomes a capability accelerator rather than a bottleneck.

McKinsey’s research reinforces this. The firm estimates a $250 billion opportunity in modernizing defense by scaling technologies such as AI, advanced simulation and quantum computing but warns that capital alone is not enough. Across defense ecosystems, the biggest bottlenecks are no longer invention, but adoption: fragmented funding, slow acquisition pathways, legacy infrastructure and limited computing capacity at the tactical edge.

Without new delivery models that prioritize software-defined systems, modular upgrades and faster transition from prototype to fielded capability, even mature technologies risk stalling before they produce operational impact.

Why Legacy Investment Models Are Misaligned

Despite all this, many defense budgets remain stuck in a 20th-century mindset. GAO assessments of major defense programs like the F-35 Joint Strike Fighter Program show that acquisition and budgeting processes remain structured around hardware delivery, with software, digital infrastructure and simulation treated as secondary investments rather than primary capability drivers.

As former Kessel Run leaders have argued, the DoD continues to apply Cold War-era acquisition models to software-defined warfare. We are still structuring budgets and procurement processes around hardware timelines rather than sustained investment in digital infrastructure and continuous delivery.

That’s a problem because hardware delivery runs on multi-year timelines, while software moves in sprints. When organizations treat code as an afterthought, they give up the agility that defines modern defense readiness.

There’s also a cultural challenge. Many organizations still see software teams as “support,” rather than as mission-critical contributors. That mindset slows innovation and discourages cross-functional collaboration.

And metrics haven’t caught up either. Readiness is still tracked in platform counts or tonnage rather than update velocity, availability or system maturity.

What Tech Leaders in Defense Should Do Now

For defense technology leaders including CTOs, engineering VPs, software architects and program managers, this shift starts with reframing the architecture. Treat your platforms as living systems. Map where simulation, software and mission-logic updates can drive more value than another round of procurement. Build modular systems that separate mission software from the hardware shell, so over-the-air updates and rapid improvements become possible.

The next step is to invest in simulation and virtual prototyping. The ability to “fail fast” in simulation is what separates the organizations that innovate continuously from those that stagnate. Digital twins and design-space exploration tools help teams test scenarios, catch flaws and make changes before the first component is ever built. Every digital failure saves time, money and human risk later on.

Measurement needs a reboot, too. Old KPIs like “jets delivered” or “hardware cost” don’t tell the story anymore. Start tracking iteration speed, update frequency and uptime. How many aircraft are running the latest build? How long does it take to push a patch to the field? These are the metrics that define digital-age readiness.

Finally, funding and supplier relationships have to evolve. Partners should be incentivized for continuous delivery, faster software cycles, sensor integrations and mission-logic refreshes, not just production volume. Shift contracts from “build once” to “iterate continuously.” In practice, that means paying for agility, not just assembly.

A Cultural Shift and a Technical One

Becoming software-first isn’t just about writing more code. It’s about changing how defense leaders think about innovation itself. Platforms can’t be treated as finished products; they must be managed as evolving ecosystems. That requires tight collaboration between design, software and operations from day one.

Flight hours and physical assets don’t tell the full story anymore. If the software running those systems is outdated, readiness is only an illusion. McKinsey’s data shows the window to modernize is narrowing as emerging technologies like AI and quantum simulation scale globally. The defense organizations that move fastest will define the next era of readiness.

Because the next breakthrough won’t roll off an assembly line. It’ll come from the cloud. It’ll come from better algorithms, faster updates and simulation-driven design. If air superiority is decided in code first, why is the budget still built for metal? The mission has changed, and so must the mindset.

BQP Unveils Hybrid Quantum-Classical Approach for CFD Simulations in Aerospace

Originally published on HPCwire

By HPCwire, NEW YORK, Sept. 12, 2024 — BQP, a startup leading the development of quantum-based engineering simulations, today announced a significant research milestone for simulating Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). The milestone was achieved using a hybrid quantum-classical solver which is part of BQP’s next-gen simulation platform, BQPhy.

After conducting ~100,000 experiments, BQP researchers published their work in a paper where they estimated that large-scale CFD simulation of a jet engine can be achieved with only 30 logical qubits on a quantum computer, leading to better accuracy, efficiency, and costs than current methods. A prior study, which inspired the BQP team to undertake this research, found that it required 19.2M compute cores to perform this same simulation with classical algorithms on state-of-the-art High-Performance Computers (HPCs).

“This study is pivotal as it would democratize large-scale CFD simulation for every engineer once quantum computers become utility-scale,” said Abhishek Chopra, founder, CEO, and Chief Scientific Officer at BQP. “In the future, what would engineers have easier access to – 19.2M HPC cores or 30-logical-qubit quantum computers? I bet on the latter.”

“With continued research, we believe that quantum computing has the potential to revolutionize the way simulations are conducted, allowing engineers to push the boundaries of design and engineering,” Chopra added.

“BQP’s results signal the introduction of drastically higher computing power to flow field analysis and simulation. This capability can unlock new methods in aerospace development, enabling higher confidence during design and more proactive maintenance during the aircraft life cycle,” said Dan Hart, Senior Aerospace Executive and Member of the National Academy of Engineering.

For the research, BQP scientists estimated scalability, accuracy, and consistency for jet engine simulations using BQP’s Hybrid Quantum Classical Finite Method (HQCFM) solver. The study demonstrated the scalability of the HQCFM solver, by simulating a non-linear time-dependent Partial Differential Equation (PDE) from 4 to all the way to 11 qubits.

Researchers found that accuracy and consistency were comparable to classical computers, while the HQCFM distinguished itself by running inside a time loop in a transient problem, without propagating any error to the next time step. Obtaining such high accuracy consistently is a significant breakthrough toward more complex simulations beyond the capacity of classical devices.

BQP believes that BQPhy’s solver will allow CFD engineers to simulate a full aircraft for the first time, allowing aerospace engineers to greatly improve flight patterns during turbulence. Given current trends in supercomputing computational advances, simulating an entire aircraft via classical computing would not be possible until 2080.

In fact, BQPhy’s physics-based solver can also be used to solve other PDEs to capture interactions in gas dynamics, traffic flow or flood waves in rivers. Combined with quantum algorithms, the technology can accurately solve complex equations with reduced hardware demands compared to traditional high-performance computing (HPC) methods, while enabling sophisticated and intricate simulations to be performed effectively.

“Building on our successful collaborations with leading academic institutions, government research agencies such as AFRL, DARPA, industry pioneers, and top academic institutes, BQP is eager to partner with organizations that share our vision for advancing quantum computing solutions,” said Chopra.

The BQP team has already made significant progress towards its next milestone which will be presented at one of the world’s largest aerospace research, development, and technology events in January 2025.

The study demonstrated the scalability of the HQCFM solver, by simulating a non-linear time-dependent Partial Differential Equation (PDE) from 4 to all the way to 11 qubits.

Researchers found that accuracy and consistency were comparable to classical computers, while the HQCFM distinguished itself by running inside a time loop in a transient problem, without propagating any error to the next time step. Obtaining such high accuracy consistently is a significant breakthrough toward more complex simulations beyond the capacity of classical devices.

BQP believes that BQPhy®’s solver will allow CFD engineers to simulate a full aircraft for the first time, allowing aerospace engineers to greatly improve flight patterns during turbulence. Given current trends in supercomputing computational advances, simulating an entire aircraft via classical computing would not be possible until 2080.

In fact, BQPhy®’s physics-based solver can also be used to solve other PDEs to capture interactions in gas dynamics, traffic flow or flood waves in rivers. Combined with quantum algorithms, the technology can accurately solve complex equations with reduced hardware demands compared to traditional high-performance computing (HPC) methods, while enabling sophisticated and intricate simulations to be performed effectively.

“Building on our successful collaborations with leading academic institutions, government research agencies such as AFRL, DARPA, industry pioneers, and top academic institutes, BQP is eager to partner with organizations that share our vision for advancing quantum computing solutions,” said Chopra.
